questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Mobile Vision Clinic
When school nurse Carla Mendes reviewed attendance records, she noticed a pattern. Several students who often complained of headaches also struggled to finish reading assignments and missed school more often than their classmates. The district had a nurse, but it did not have an eye doctor on staff. Many families could not easily schedule outside appointments because of cost, transportation, or work hours.
The district partnered with a nonprofit health group to bring a mobile vision clinic to three schools. The clinic was a bus equipped with exam chairs, eye charts, and basic lens-making equipment. Students whose parents signed permission forms received vision screenings during the school day. Those who needed a full exam could receive one on the bus, and some students left with glasses the same afternoon.
The program did not diagnose every learning problem. Teachers still needed to address reading skills, attendance barriers, and classroom support. But the clinic helped identify a basic obstacle that had been easy to miss. A student who cannot see the board clearly may seem distracted, tired, or unwilling to read.
After the first semester, teachers reported that several students with new glasses participated more during lessons. One eighth grader told Mendes that he had thought everyone saw words “with fuzzy edges.” The mobile clinic showed that a health service brought directly to students could remove a barrier before it became a larger academic problem.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A mobile vision clinic helped the district identify and address vision problems that affected some students’ learning.
choices.B: The district replaced all classroom instruction with eye exams.
choices.C: Students with headaches were told to stop attending reading class.
choices.D: The mobile clinic diagnosed every possible learning problem in the district.

questions.correctAnswerText: A mobile vision clinic helped the district identify and address vision problems that affected some students’ learning.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how the clinic brought eye care to students and removed a barrier to learning for some of them.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mobile Vision Clinic.”
Why did the district bring the clinic to schools?

choices.A: Many families faced cost, transportation, or scheduling barriers to outside eye appointments.
choices.B: The district wanted to stop using permission forms.
choices.C: Teachers asked students to make glasses during class.
choices.D: The school nurse no longer wanted to review attendance records.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many families faced cost, transportation, or scheduling barriers to outside eye appointments.
questions.explanation: These barriers explain why the district partnered with a mobile clinic.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Mobile Vision Clinic.”
Which TWO services did the mobile clinic provide?

choices.A: Vision screenings during the school day
choices.B: Full eye exams for some students
choices.C: Surgery for every student with headaches
choices.D: Transportation to outside hospitals

questions.correctAnswerText: Vision screenings during the school day | Full eye exams for some students
questions.explanation: The passage says students received screenings and some received full exams on the bus.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mobile Vision Clinic.”
What can be inferred from the eighth grader’s comment about seeing words “with fuzzy edges”?

choices.A: He may not have realized that his vision was different from what others experienced.
choices.B: He was refusing to wear his new glasses.
choices.C: He believed reading assignments should be removed from school.
choices.D: He had already received regular eye exams every year.

questions.correctAnswerText: He may not have realized that his vision was different from what others experienced.
questions.explanation: The comment suggests the student thought blurry words were normal.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Mobile Vision Clinic.”
In the sentence “the clinic helped identify a basic obstacle that had been easy to miss,” obstacle most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=barrier or difficulty
questions.explanation: In context, obstacle means a barrier that makes learning more difficult.

Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Students whose parents signed permission forms received screenings.
tiles.B: The clinic was a bus equipped with exam chairs and eye charts.
tiles.C: Teachers reported that several students with new glasses participated more.
tiles.D: The clinic replaced all reading instruction.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Forklift Battery Charging Procedure
To: Warehouse Associates and Shift Leads
From: Operations Supervisor
Subject: Forklift Battery Charging Procedure
Beginning January 12, all electric forklift batteries must be charged only in the marked charging area at the west end of the warehouse. The area has ventilation, spill-control supplies, and posted emergency instructions.
Before charging a battery, employees must inspect the charging cable for cracks, exposed wires, or loose connectors. If damage is found, do not use the charger. Place an “Out of Service” tag on it and notify a shift lead immediately. Employees should also make sure the forklift is turned off and the parking brake is set before connecting the charger.
Only trained employees may connect or disconnect forklift batteries. New employees may observe the process during training, but they may not perform the task alone until a shift lead has signed their training checklist. Safety goggles and gloves must be worn when working near batteries.
Charging logs must be completed at the end of each charging session. The log should include the forklift number, start time, end time, employee initials, and any problems noticed. Accurate logs help maintenance staff identify equipment issues before they interrupt warehouse operations.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain safe steps for charging and recording electric forklift batteries
choices.B: To tell employees to charge batteries anywhere in the warehouse
choices.C: To allow new employees to charge forklifts without training
choices.D: To announce that charging logs are no longer needed

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain safe steps for charging and recording electric forklift batteries
questions.explanation: The document gives procedures for safe charging, inspection, training, protective equipment, and logs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Forklift Battery Charging Procedure.”
What should an employee do if a charging cable has exposed wires?

choices.A: Tag the charger “Out of Service” and notify a shift lead.
choices.B: Continue charging the battery if the forklift is needed.
choices.C: Cover the wire with paper and finish the session.
choices.D: Ask a new employee to connect the battery.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tag the charger “Out of Service” and notify a shift lead.
questions.explanation: The document instructs employees not to use damaged chargers and to notify a shift lead.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Forklift Battery Charging Procedure.”
Which TWO details must be included in the charging log?

choices.A: The forklift number
choices.B: The employee initials
choices.C: The employee’s lunch order
choices.D: The customer’s delivery address
choices.E: The color of the warehouse floor

questions.correctAnswerText: The forklift number | The employee initials
questions.explanation: Both details are required in the charging log.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Forklift Battery Charging Procedure.”
Why must charging be done in the marked charging area?

choices.A: The area has ventilation, spill-control supplies, and emergency instructions.
choices.B: The area is closest to the employee break room.
choices.C: The area allows untrained visitors to charge forklifts.
choices.D: The area prevents shift leads from checking equipment.

questions.correctAnswerText: The area has ventilation, spill-control supplies, and emergency instructions.
questions.explanation: These safety features explain why charging must happen in the marked area.

choices.A: “Beginning January 12, all electric forklift batteries must be charged only in the marked charging area.”
choices.B: “Employees should also make sure the forklift is turned off and the parking brake is set.”
choices.C: “Accurate logs help maintenance staff identify equipment issues before they interrupt warehouse operations.”
choices.D: “New employees may observe the process during training.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Accurate logs help maintenance staff identify equipment issues before they interrupt warehouse operations.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains why charging logs matter.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Create a City Tool-Lending Library for Home Energy Savings
The city should create a tool-lending library focused on home energy savings. Many residents want to lower utility bills but do not own the basic tools needed to find drafts, seal gaps, or install simple efficiency upgrades. A lending program could offer items such as caulk guns, thermal leak detectors, weather-stripping tools, outlet testers, and instructional guides.
The program would be especially useful for residents who cannot justify buying a tool they may use once or twice. A caulk gun or leak detector may be inexpensive to some people, but every extra cost matters for households already struggling with heating or cooling bills. Borrowing tools for a weekend could help residents complete small projects without adding another expense.
The library should include training, not just equipment. Short demonstrations could show residents how to use tools safely, where air leaks commonly appear, and when a problem requires a licensed professional. Borrowers could sign an agreement, return tools by a deadline, and report damaged equipment.
Some critics may say energy savings should be handled by contractors or utility companies. Those services are important, but they do not reach everyone quickly. A tool-lending library would not replace professional weatherization programs. It would give residents a practical way to take simple first steps while they wait for larger help or decide what work is needed.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: A city tool-lending library could help residents complete simple home energy-saving projects affordably.
choices.B: Residents should stop using professional contractors for every home repair.
choices.C: The city should require every household to buy thermal leak detectors.
choices.D: Utility companies should stop offering weatherization programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: A city tool-lending library could help residents complete simple home energy-saving projects affordably.
questions.explanation: The author argues that lending tools and offering guidance would lower barriers to small energy-saving projects.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Create a City Tool-Lending Library for Home Energy Savings.”

choices.B: Borrowers could sign an agreement.
choices.C: Professional weatherization programs are important.
choices.D: Some problems require licensed professionals.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some residents cannot justify buying a tool they may use only once or twice.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the idea that borrowing tools can reduce costs.

choices.B: Borrower agreements and return deadlines
choices.C: Unlimited borrowing with no record of damaged tools
choices.D: Requiring residents to perform licensed electrical work
choices.E: Removing instructional guides from the program

questions.correctAnswerText: Short demonstrations on safe tool use | Borrower agreements and return deadlines
questions.explanation: The author recommends training, agreements, and return deadlines for responsible use.

choices.A: By arguing that those services are useful but may not reach everyone quickly
choices.B: By claiming professional services are always unnecessary
choices.C: By saying residents should never ask for larger help
choices.D: By recommending that the city ban utility programs

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that those services are useful but may not reach everyone quickly
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges professional programs but says a tool library can fill a gap.

questions.correct: D1=A local housing survey found that many residents delayed weather-sealing projects because they lacked tools or were unsure which supplies to buy.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A local housing survey found that many residents delayed weather-sealing projects because they lacked tools or were unsure which supplies to buy.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by showing that lack of tools and knowledge delays projects.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Create a City Tool-Lending Library for Home Energy Savings.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some residents can complete simple energy-saving tasks if they have access to tools and basic guidance.
choices.B: Every home energy problem can be solved without professional help.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Measuring Spoon
Rafael had worked in the school cafeteria since September, mostly washing trays and restocking milk cartons. On Fridays, when the kitchen smelled like cinnamon and warm apples, he watched Ms. Donnelly make the oatmeal bars that disappeared faster than anything else on the lunch line.
One morning she handed him a clipboard and a measuring spoon. “You’re mixing the dry ingredients today,” she said.
Rafael looked at the recipe. It was written for two hundred servings. The numbers seemed too large to belong in one bowl. “What if I ruin it?”
“Then we learn before lunch,” Ms. Donnelly said.
He measured oats, flour, cinnamon, and baking powder. The first scoop of cinnamon rose above the spoon in a small hill. He started to shake it into the bowl, but Ms. Donnelly stopped him. “Level means level. Recipes are instructions, not guesses.”
Rafael scraped the spoon flat against the container’s edge and tried again. He expected the work to feel smaller once he understood it, but it felt bigger. Every scoop mattered because every student would taste the result.
At lunch, the oatmeal bars held together. They were not as neat as Ms. Donnelly’s, but they were sweet, warm, and gone before the last class reached the line. Rafael found the empty pan after cleanup and ran one finger along the corner where crumbs had stuck. He had not ruined anything. He had measured his way into the recipe.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Rafael must overcome uncertainty as he takes responsibility for part of a large recipe.
choices.B: Rafael tries to convince Ms. Donnelly to stop serving lunch.
choices.C: Rafael argues with students about milk cartons.
choices.D: Rafael refuses to follow any recipe instructions.

questions.correctAnswerText: Rafael must overcome uncertainty as he takes responsibility for part of a large recipe.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Rafael’s worry and his careful completion of the recipe task.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Measuring Spoon.”

choices.B: He cannot read any of the words on the page.
choices.C: Ms. Donnelly has already thrown away the ingredients.
choices.D: The students have refused to eat oatmeal bars.

questions.correctAnswerText: The recipe is written for many servings, making the responsibility feel large.
questions.explanation: Rafael feels intimidated because the recipe is for two hundred servings.

choices.B: Rafael should ignore the recipe and use his own ideas.
choices.C: Recipes are useful only after lunch is over.
choices.D: Cinnamon should never be used in cafeteria food.

questions.correctAnswerText: Rafael should measure carefully instead of estimating.
questions.explanation: Ms. Donnelly’s instruction means the recipe requires accuracy.

choices.D: He refuses to look at the clipboard again.
choices.E: He leaves the kitchen before cleanup.

questions.correctAnswerText: He scrapes the measuring spoon flat before adding cinnamon. | He realizes every student will taste the result.
questions.explanation: These details show Rafael learning precision and understanding responsibility.

choices.A: Rafael has gained confidence by carefully completing a real responsibility.
choices.B: Rafael plans to stop working in the cafeteria.

choices.D: Rafael thinks the oatmeal bars failed.

questions.correctAnswerText: Rafael has gained confidence by carefully completing a real responsibility.
questions.explanation: The final sentence suggests Rafael has become part of the cooking process through careful work.

questions.explanation: The tiles show doubt, guidance, and a successful result.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
What a Product Recall Means
A product recall is a request from a company or safety agency for customers to stop using a product because it may be unsafe or defective. Recalls may involve vehicles, appliances, toys, food, medicine, tools, or household items. Some recalls are announced after injuries occur, while others happen because testing reveals a possible risk.
A recall notice usually explains the product name, model number, date range, hazard, and steps customers should take. The solution may be a repair, replacement, refund, software update, or disposal instruction. Customers should read the notice carefully because two products that look similar may have different model numbers, and only one may be included in the recall.
It is important not to ignore a recall simply because the product has worked normally so far. A slow-developing electrical problem, weak part, or contamination issue may not be obvious at first. Continuing to use the product can create a risk even if nothing has happened yet.
Customers can respond more easily if they keep purchase records, register products when appropriate, and check official recall sources. Registration cards and online forms are sometimes used to contact owners directly. A recall does not mean every product has already caused harm. It means enough concern exists that customers should follow the instructions before the risk becomes real.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Product recalls warn customers about possible risks and explain what steps to take.
choices.B: Recalls apply only to vehicles and never to household items.
choices.C: Customers should ignore a recall if the product seems to work normally.
choices.D: A recall means every product has already injured someone.

questions.correctAnswerText: Product recalls warn customers about possible risks and explain what steps to take.
questions.explanation: The passage explains what recalls are, what notices include, and how customers should respond.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What a Product Recall Means.”
Why should customers check the model number in a recall notice?

choices.A: Similar-looking products may not all be included in the recall.
choices.B: Model numbers are used only to determine product color.
choices.C: Every model number always has the same safety problem.
choices.D: The model number replaces the need to read the hazard description.

questions.correctAnswerText: Similar-looking products may not all be included in the recall.
questions.explanation: The passage says customers should compare model numbers because only some similar-looking products may be recalled.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What a Product Recall Means.”
Which action may be required by a recall notice?

choices.A: Repairing, replacing, returning, updating, or disposing of the product
choices.B: Continuing to use the product without checking it
choices.C: Removing all labels from the product
choices.D: Selling the product to another customer immediately

questions.correctAnswerText: Repairing, replacing, returning, updating, or disposing of the product
questions.explanation: The passage lists these possible recall solutions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What a Product Recall Means.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that a product can be risky even before a customer notices a problem?

choices.A: “Recalls may involve vehicles, appliances, toys, food, medicine, tools, or household items.”
choices.B: “A slow-developing electrical problem, weak part, or contamination issue may not be obvious at first.”
choices.C: “The solution may be a repair, replacement, refund, software update, or disposal instruction.”
choices.D: “Registration cards and online forms are sometimes used to contact owners directly.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A slow-developing electrical problem, weak part, or contamination issue may not be obvious at first.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains why a product may be risky before a visible problem appears.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: It defines recalls, explains what notices include, and gives advice for responding.
choices.B: It tells a story about one family returning a toy.
choices.C: It ranks recalled products from most dangerous to least dangerous.
choices.D: It argues that companies should never test products.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines recalls, explains what notices include, and gives advice for responding.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from definition to notice details to response advice.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Neighborhood Flu Clinic
(1) The West Field Health Center will hold a flu clinic at the high school gym next Saturday. (2) Patients should bring an insurance card if they have one, but no one will be turned away for lack of insurance. (3) Staff will provide consent forms at the entrance. (4) [D1], patients can download the form in advance and bring a completed copy. (5) The clinic [D2] open from 10:00 a.m. until 2:00 p.m. (6) Volunteers will guide patients from check-in to the observation area.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Alternatively; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “Alternatively” correctly introduces another way to get the form, and “will be open” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Neighborhood Flu Clinic.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Volunteers will guide patients from check-in to the observation area.
choices.B: Volunteers will be the people who guide patients from the place of check-in to the area used for observation.
choices.C: Patients will be guided by volunteers, who are volunteers, from check-in to observation.
choices.D: The guiding of patients from check-in to the observation area will be done by volunteers.

questions.correctAnswerText: Volunteers will guide patients from check-in to the observation area.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and keeps the original meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The boxes of consent forms is beside the entrance.
choices.B: The group of nurses are ready for the clinic.
choices.C: The volunteers who greet patients wear yellow badges.
choices.D: The schedule for appointment slots have been posted.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers who greet patients wear yellow badges.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “volunteers” correctly takes the plural verb “wear.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Elena texted Brooke after the clinic, she thanked the volunteers.
choices.B: Elena texted Brooke after the clinic, and Elena thanked the volunteers.
choices.C: She thanked them when Elena texted Brooke after the clinic.
choices.D: After the clinic, she texted her and thanked them.

questions.correctAnswerText: Elena texted Brooke after the clinic, and Elena thanked the volunteers.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Elena” makes clear who thanked the volunteers.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The clinic expected many walk-in patients. [D1], staff prepared extra chairs for the waiting area.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore
questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows that extra chairs were prepared because many walk-ins were expected.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The clinic opened at ten; however, several patients arrived early.
choices.B: The clinic opened at ten however several patients arrived early.
choices.C: The clinic opened at ten, however several patients arrived early.
choices.D: The clinic opened at ten; however several patients, arrived early.

questions.correctAnswerText: The clinic opened at ten; however, several patients arrived early.
questions.explanation: A semicolon before “however” and a comma after it correctly join the two independent clauses.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the gym doors opened at ten.
choices.B: After patients completed the consent form.
choices.C: The nurse reviewed the form before the vaccination.
choices.D: While waiting in the observation area.

questions.correctAnswerText: The nurse reviewed the form before the vaccination.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The assistant checked the supply table and restocks the bandages yesterday.
choices.B: The assistant checks the supply table and restocked the bandages yesterday.
choices.C: The assistant checked the supply table and restocked the bandages yesterday.
choices.D: The assistant had checked the supply table and restocks the bandages yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The assistant checked the supply table and restocked the bandages yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The checklist includes patient names, appointment times, and whether forms are complete.
choices.B: The checklist includes patient names, appointment times, and form status.
choices.C: The checklist includes naming patients, appointment times, and to complete forms.
choices.D: The checklist includes patient names, times for appointments, and forms that are complete.

questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Add a Community Kitchen Incubator
The city should create a community kitchen incubator for small food entrepreneurs. Many residents know how to make sauces, baked goods, prepared meals, or specialty snacks, but they cannot legally sell food made in a home kitchen if it does not meet safety rules. A shared commercial kitchen would give beginners access to approved equipment without requiring them to rent a full restaurant space.
The incubator could support local business growth. Participants could reserve kitchen time, complete food-safety training, receive help with packaging rules, and test products at approved community events. A person who wants to start small should not have to invest thousands of dollars before learning whether customers are interested.
The city could manage risk through rules. Users would need permits, training, cleaning checklists, and scheduled inspections. The kitchen could begin with limited hours and a small number of participants. A well-run incubator would not guarantee business success, but it would lower the first barrier for residents with serious food-business ideas.
Passage B: Avoid Running a City Food Business Kitchen
The city should not operate a community kitchen incubator. Starting a food business is difficult, and a public kitchen may create expectations that the city cannot meet. The city would need to pay for equipment, maintenance, utilities, inspections, scheduling, insurance, and staff oversight. If the kitchen is not managed carefully, food safety problems could harm customers and damage the city’s reputation.
There is also a fairness issue. Many restaurants, bakeries, and caterers already pay rent and follow strict rules. A city-supported kitchen could give some new sellers an advantage while established businesses continue carrying higher costs. Public funds should not be used to compete with businesses that have already invested in the community.
The city can support food entrepreneurs in less risky ways. It can offer workshops on permits, connect residents with existing commercial kitchens, and create a guide to farmers market rules. These services would provide useful information without making the city responsible for operating a food production site.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports a shared kitchen to help new food businesses start, while Passage B argues the city should avoid operating a costly and risky food production site.
choices.B: Passage A argues that home kitchens should never follow safety rules, while Passage B argues that restaurants should stop selling food.
choices.C: Passage A supports closing farmers markets, while Passage B supports banning packaged foods.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on school lunches, while Passage B focuses on apartment repairs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports a shared kitchen to help new food businesses start, while Passage B argues the city should avoid operating a costly and risky food production site.
questions.explanation: Passage A emphasizes access for entrepreneurs, while Passage B emphasizes cost, safety, and fairness risks.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about a community kitchen incubator.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: A shared commercial kitchen would give beginners access to approved equipment without requiring full restaurant space.
choices.B: The city would need to pay for maintenance and insurance.
choices.C: Established restaurants already pay rent.
choices.D: Food safety problems could harm customers.

questions.correctAnswerText: A shared commercial kitchen would give beginners access to approved equipment without requiring full restaurant space.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument that an incubator would lower startup barriers.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about a community kitchen incubator.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Operating the kitchen would require equipment, utilities, inspections, insurance, and staff oversight.
choices.B: A city-supported kitchen could create fairness concerns for established food businesses.
choices.C: No resident has ever wanted to start a food business.
choices.D: Food-safety training is always unnecessary.
choices.E: A public kitchen would guarantee that every participant becomes successful.

questions.correctAnswerText: Operating the kitchen would require equipment, utilities, inspections, insurance, and staff oversight. | A city-supported kitchen could create fairness concerns for established food businesses.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims with specific reasons.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about a community kitchen incubator.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses safety or management concerns?

choices.A: “Many residents know how to make sauces, baked goods, prepared meals, or specialty snacks.”
choices.B: “A person who wants to start small should not have to invest thousands of dollars.”
choices.C: “Users would need permits, training, cleaning checklists, and scheduled inspections.”
choices.D: “The city should create a community kitchen incubator for small food entrepreneurs.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Users would need permits, training, cleaning checklists, and scheduled inspections.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly addresses safety and management concerns.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about a community kitchen incubator.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors recognize that food businesses require safety rules and support, but they disagree about whether the city should operate a kitchen.
choices.B: Both authors believe residents should sell food without permits.
choices.C: Both authors argue that established restaurants should close.
choices.D: Both authors think packaging rules are unrelated to food businesses.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors recognize that food businesses require safety rules and support, but they disagree about whether the city should operate a kitchen.
questions.explanation: Both passages discuss food business support and safety, but they reach different conclusions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about a community kitchen incubator.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Estimated operating costs, demand from food entrepreneurs, and availability of existing commercial kitchens
choices.B: A list of favorite desserts among city employees
choices.C: A history of grocery store architecture
choices.D: A survey about the color of kitchen walls

questions.correctAnswerText: Estimated operating costs, demand from food entrepreneurs, and availability of existing commercial kitchens
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate the need, cost, and alternatives.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Stored in refrigerated boxes, Hannah carried the vaccine doses to the clinic table.

choices.A: Stored in refrigerated boxes, Hannah carried the vaccine doses to the clinic table.
choices.B: Hannah carried the vaccine doses stored in refrigerated boxes to the clinic table.
choices.C: Hannah, stored in refrigerated boxes, carried the vaccine doses to the clinic table.
choices.D: The clinic table carried Hannah’s refrigerated vaccine boxes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Hannah carried the vaccine doses stored in refrigerated boxes to the clinic table.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “vaccine doses,” the items stored in refrigerated boxes.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The health center sent a reminder. The reminder listed the clinic hours.

choices.A: The health center sent a reminder that listed the clinic hours.
choices.B: The reminder sent the health center and listed the clinic hours.
choices.C: Listing the health center, the clinic hours sent a reminder.
choices.D: The health center sent, and the clinic hours listed the reminder.

questions.correctAnswerText: The health center sent a reminder that listed the clinic hours.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The patient’s form was placed in the correct folder.
choices.B: The patients form was placed in the correct folder.
choices.C: The patients’ form’s was placed in the correct folder.
choices.D: The patient forms’ was placed in the correct folder.

questions.correctAnswerText: The patient’s form was placed in the correct folder.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the form belongs to one patient.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Backup Power for Senior Apartment Buildings
The city should require large senior apartment buildings to have backup power for elevators, hallway lights, and one common cooling or warming room. During long power outages, older residents may be unable to safely leave upper floors, keep medical devices charged, or remain comfortable in extreme temperatures. A backup power requirement would protect residents who are especially vulnerable when normal building systems fail.
The requirement does not need to power every apartment. It could focus on essential shared systems: one elevator, emergency lighting, charging outlets in a common room, and temperature control in that room. Buildings could use generators, battery systems, or other approved technology. The city could give owners several years to comply and offer low-interest loans for upgrades.
Some owners may object to the cost, but safety requirements are already part of operating housing. Buildings must follow fire codes, maintain exits, and keep basic systems in working order. Backup power for essential services is a similar responsibility in a time when severe storms and heat waves can leave residents at risk for hours or days.
A backup power rule would not solve every emergency problem, but it would give residents a safer place to wait for help and a better chance of staying connected during an outage.
Passage B:
Do Not Mandate Backup Power Yet
The city should not immediately require senior apartment buildings to install backup power systems. The goal is important, but a mandate could create serious financial pressure, especially for older buildings with limited budgets. Generators, batteries, wiring upgrades, fuel storage, inspections, and maintenance can be expensive. If costs are passed to residents through higher rents or fees, the policy could harm the people it is meant to protect.
The city should first identify which buildings are most at risk. Some buildings may already have partial backup systems. Others may have only a few floors or residents with strong support networks. A single requirement for all large senior buildings may ignore important differences. The city should conduct an assessment before setting deadlines.
There are immediate steps that may improve safety while longer-term plans are developed. Buildings can create outage checklists, maintain resident contact lists, provide portable charging stations, coordinate with emergency services, and identify nearby cooling or warming centers. These steps cost less and can be put in place quickly.
Backup power may be a good goal, but the city should study costs, risks, and funding options before creating a mandate.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether the city should require backup power for large senior apartment buildings? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
